title: REST API Design
description: Endpoints, requests, responses. Design APIs that are easy to use and maintain.

learningObjectives.text: Define what a REST API is and when to build one
Design clean endpoints using REST conventions
Handle authentication and rate limiting in an API

contentSections.title: When does your tool need an API?

contentSections.body: You built a web app. It works. Users love it. Then someone asks: 'Can I integrate this into my own app?' That's when you need an API. An API turns your tool from a product into a platform — other apps can use what you built.

### REST API basics

REST uses HTTP verbs and URLs to describe operations:

```
GET    /api/summaries          → list all summaries
GET    /api/summaries/abc123   → get one summary
POST   /api/summaries          → create a summary (with body)
DELETE /api/summaries/abc123   → delete a summary
```

**Naming conventions:**
- Plural nouns for resources: `/users`, `/documents`, `/summaries`
- No verbs in URLs: `/api/createSummary` ❌ → `/api/summaries` + POST ✓
- Nest related resources: `/users/:id/documents`
- Return consistent JSON structure

contentSections.question: What HTTP status code should you return when a resource is successfully created?
contentSections.answer: **201 Created** (not 200 OK). HTTP status codes are semantic: 200 = OK (general success), 201 = Created (new resource made), 400 = Bad Request (client error), 401 = Unauthorized (no auth), 403 = Forbidden (auth but no permission), 404 = Not Found, 429 = Rate Limited, 500 = Server Error.
contentSections.hint: HTTP has specific status codes for different success scenarios. 200 is general success, but there's a more specific one for creation.

contentSections.body: Use the right HTTP verbs (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) and status codes (200/201/400/401/404/500). This is what 'REST' means in practice.
**Never store API keys in plaintext.** Store the SHA-256 hash. Even if your database is breached, attackers can't use hashed keys. This is how GitHub, Stripe, and Anthropic handle it.

title: Charge for Your Tool
description: Stripe integration. Add payment processing without the complexity.

learningObjectives.text: Set up Stripe and create a payment link
Implement a subscription with Stripe Checkout
Handle webhooks to update user access after payment

contentSections.body: The hardest part of charging for a tool isn't the code — it's deciding to charge at all. Once you've decided, Stripe makes the actual implementation straightforward. You can go from 'free' to 'paid subscriptions' in an afternoon.

### Three ways to charge with Stripe

**1. Payment Link (no code):**
Create in Stripe dashboard → share URL → user pays → you see money. Zero code. Good for testing if people will pay at all.

**2. Stripe Checkout (minimal code):**
Redirect users to Stripe's hosted checkout page. You handle the redirect and the webhook. Good for most tools.

**3. Stripe Elements (custom UI):**
Embed the payment form directly in your app. More control, more code. Use this when Checkout's design doesn't fit.

Start with Checkout.

contentSections.question: User pays on Stripe's hosted page. How does your backend know the payment succeeded?
contentSections.answer: **Webhooks.** Stripe sends an HTTP POST to your server with a signed event payload when something happens (payment succeeded, subscription created, payment failed). Your backend receives the event, verifies the signature, and updates the user's access. This is the standard pattern for all payment processing.
contentSections.hint: The user is on Stripe's page, not yours. How does your server learn what happened?

contentSections.body: Webhooks are how payment platforms talk back to your server. Always verify the webhook signature — don't trust unverified POST requests.
**Create prices in the Stripe dashboard, not in code.** Use price IDs in your env vars. This lets you change prices without redeploying.

title: Analytics & Iteration
description: Track what users actually do. Use data to improve what matters.

learningObjectives.text: Define which metrics matter for a tool builder
Implement basic usage tracking in an Express API
Use data to prioritize what to build next

contentSections.body: You think users want feature X. Your data shows 90% of them never go past the first screen. Without analytics, you're guessing. With analytics, you're building the thing people actually use.

### The 3 metrics that matter most

**1. Activation:** Did new users complete their first successful action?
(They signed up → ran the tool once → got a result)

**2. Retention:** Do users come back?
(Week 1 → Week 2 → Week 4 active users)

**3. Revenue per user:** Are paying users using it?
(Cancelled subs that are high-usage = pricing issue. Paying subs that are low-usage = churn risk.)

Everything else is a vanity metric.

contentSections.question: 1000 users signed up last month. 900 never ran the tool. What's the most important thing to fix?
contentSections.answer: **The activation flow.** 90% of users can't (or won't) complete their first action. Before optimizing pricing, adding features, or running ads, fix the gap between 'signed up' and 'first value.' This is usually: the UI is confusing, the tool requires too much setup, or the first result is disappointing.
contentSections.hint: Which metric is broken here — activation, retention, or revenue?

contentSections.body: Fix activation before retention. Fix retention before revenue. In that order.
**PostHog** is free, open-source, and has a generous free tier. For most tools, it's better than rolling your own. `npm install posthog-node`

### Questions your data should answer

- **What do users do first?** → Your most important feature
- **Where do they drop off?** → Your biggest friction point
- **What do paying users do differently?** → What drives conversion
- **What causes cancellations?** → What to fix immediately

### The iteration cycle:
1. Look at your data (1 hour/week)
2. Form a hypothesis: 'If I [change X], [metric Y] will improve'
3. Build the smallest version of the change
4. Measure for 1 week
5. Keep or revert based on data

Most tool builders look at their data too rarely (monthly). Weekly is the minimum.

title: Scaling Without Breaking
description: Rate limiting, caching, cost optimization. Keep your tool fast and affordable as it grows.

learningObjectives.text: Estimate and monitor Claude API costs
Implement caching to reduce API calls and costs
Design rate limiting to protect against abuse

contentSections.body: Your tool works great with 10 users. Then you get 1000. Suddenly your Claude API bill is $400/month and climbing. Scaling without breaking means planning for cost and abuse before they become emergencies.

### Estimating Claude costs

Claude charges per token (roughly 4 characters = 1 token).

Haiku pricing: ~$0.25/M input tokens, ~$1.25/M output tokens
Sonnet pricing: ~$3/M input tokens, ~$15/M output tokens

### Example: summarizer tool
- Average input: 2000 tokens ($0.0005 with Haiku)
- Average output: 200 tokens ($0.00025)
- Cost per request: ~$0.00075
- 1000 users × 5 requests/day = 5000 req/day = $3.75/day = ~$112/month

At $12/month subscription, 10 users = $120 revenue. $112 cost. That's barely breaking even. **Model selection matters.**

contentSections.question: Your summarizer costs $0.00075/request with Haiku. 100 users run it 10 times/day = 1000 req/day. What would it cost with Sonnet instead?
contentSections.answer: **~$15/day vs $0.75/day.** Sonnet is ~20× more expensive than Haiku. For a summarizer, Haiku is usually good enough. Use Sonnet only where output quality meaningfully matters (complex analysis, code review) and where users will pay a premium for it.
contentSections.hint: Sonnet costs ~20× more per token than Haiku.

contentSections.body: Default to Haiku. Upgrade to Sonnet only where quality delta is user-visible and worth the cost.
